coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer for specific recognition of sterigmatocystin in grains
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Longhua
Fang, Guozhen
Pan, Mingfei
Wang, Xuefeng
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A novel sensitive fluorescent sensor for determination of sterigmatocystin (ST), which was based on carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@MIP), was prepared by an efficient one-pot reaction. First, highly blue luminescent CDs were synthesized via a one-step reaction. Then, through a non-hydrolytic sol–gel process, MIP was formed on the CDs surface in the presence of 1,8-dihydroxyanthraquinone as an alternative template molecule to obtain CDs@MIP. The CDs acted as antennas for signal amplification and optical readout, and the MIP coated on the CDs surface provided specific binding sites for ST. The performance of CDs@MIP was compared with that of CDs embedded in non-imprinted polymer (CDs@NIP). CDs@MIP exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity toward ST. Under optimized conditions, the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP decreased linearly with the concentration of ST from 0.05 to 2.0mgL−1 with a detection limit of 0.019mgL−1 (S/N=3) and the precision for five replicate detections of 0.10mgL−1 ST was 2.31%. The sensor was also used to determine the content of ST in grains with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Non-hydrolytic sol–gel
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Sterigmatocystin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of silica-based hybrid MIP- and NIP-coated CDs
Detection of ST in real samples using CDs@MIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of (a) AEAPMS and (b) CDs; (B) Absorption and PL emission spectra of CDs.

SEM images of (A) CDs@MIP and (B) CDs@NIP; (C) FT-IR spectra of (a) DT, CDs@MIP (b) before and (c) after extraction, and (d) CDs@NIP after extraction; (D) (a) UV–vis absorption, (b) FL excitation and (c) emission spectra of CDs@MIP.
(A) FL spectra of (a) CDs@NIP, CDs@MIP (b) before and (c) after extraction and (d) CDs@MIP with addition of ST (1.0mgL−1); (B) Stability of FL emission of CDs@MIP.

(A) Selectivity of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP for DT, QC, OTA, ST, AFB1 and ZEN; (B) Effect of the competitive analog ZEN in the recognition of template ST on CDs@MIP; FL spectra of (C) CDs@MIP and (D) CDs@NIP upon addition of the indicated concentration of ST. Error bars are from three parallel experiments.
Recovery results for the determination of ST in grain samples using CDs@MIP.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer for specific recognition of sterigmatocystin in grains

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Yunnan Agricultural University, Kunming 650201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology, Yunnan Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sensitive fluorescent sensor for determination of sterigmatocystin (ST), which was based on carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@MIP), was prepared by an efficient one-pot reaction. First, highly blue luminescent CDs were synthesized via a one-step reaction. Then, through a non-hydrolytic sol–gel process, MIP was formed on the CDs surface in the presence of 1,8-dihydroxyanthraquinone as an alternative template molecule to obtain CDs@MIP. The CDs acted as antennas for signal amplification and optical readout, and the MIP coated on the CDs surface provided specific binding sites for ST. The performance of CDs@MIP was compared with that of CDs embedded in non-imprinted polymer (CDs@NIP). CDs@MIP exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity toward ST. Under optimized conditions, the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP decreased linearly with the concentration of ST from 0.05 to 2.0mgL−1 with a detection limit of 0.019mgL−1 (S/N=3) and the precision for five replicate detections of 0.10mgL−1 ST was 2.31%. The sensor was also used to determine the content of ST in grains with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Sterigmatocystin (ST), a fungal secondary metabolite produced by many different Aspergillus species (Atalla et al., 2003), is a precursor of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). ST has a similar 8,9-double bond structure to that of AFB1, as shown in Fig. S1 (Georgianna and Payne, 2009). ST is widely distributed in a variety of food and feed sources (Flores-Flores et al., 2015; Veršilovskis et al., 2008), especially in grains such as wheat, corn and millet, and can pose a serious health risk to humans and livestock that consume contaminated foods (Marin et al., 2013). A large number of studies have indicated that ST affects liver and kidney function. In addition, ST has been closely correlated with the occurrence of esophagus, gastric, and hepatocellular carcinomas (Purchase and van der Watt, 1970; Steyn, 1995). The International Agency for Research on Cancer has classified ST as a group 2B carcinogen (Castegnaro and Wild, 1995) because of its potential carcinogenicity, mutagenicity and teratogenicity to humans (Mori et al., 1986; Ueda et al., 1984). However, very little data concerning monitoring of ST is available at present. Therefore, an accurate and reliable analytical method is needed to facilitate the risk assessment of human exposure to ST, and more importantly, to monitor food products for existing or future legislation.

In the past few decades, a variety of methods, including the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Li et al., 1996; Morgan et al., 1986), thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Gimeno, 1979; Josefsson and Moller, 1977; Lee et al., 1980), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Frisvad, 1989; Stack et al., 1976), as well as liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (LC–MS) and tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) (Arroyo-Manzanares et al., 2013; Jia et al., 2014; Malachova et al., 2014; Njumbe Ediage et al., 2015; Rubert et al., 2012; Veršilovskis et al., 2007) have been developed for the detection of ST. TLC methods are the most frequently used, but suffer from insufficient selectivity in complex food matrices, and thus are usually employed as qualitative screening tools (Turner et al., 2009). Modern analysis of ST heavily relies on HPLC with ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) or fluorescence (FL) detectors, which generally requires tedious sample pretreatment or complicated derivatization. LC–MS/MS methods with high sensitivity and selectivity allow qualitative screening and quantitative analysis of ST (Veršilovskis and De Saeger, 2010) but need expensive equipment and highly trained operators, which limits their application. These aforementioned methods have made great progress in the detection of ST; however, when faced with complex food matrices together with low concentrations of contaminants, they are limited by factors like slow response, high cost, and laborious pretreatment. Thus, a simple, selective and sensitive analytical method for detection of trace ST is still required.

Molecular imprinting is an effective strategy to form recognition sites with a predetermined selectivity for template molecules (Ma et al., 2013) and their analogs in complex samples. The traditional technology for preparing molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) uses an organic polymer-based system because of the availability of a variety of monomers and excellent stability of such systems at different pH. However, the system may swell or shrink when exposed to different solvents, which may considerably change the morphology of the polymer network (size and shape) and relative positions of the functional groups that are essential for recognition (Yu and Mosbach, 2000). An interesting methodology to prepare silica-based hybrid MIPs based on the non-hydrolytic sol–gel (NHSG) process offers the potential to overcome the above shortcomings, and decrease nonselective absorbance, as well as subtly increase selectivity (Wang et al., 2006, 2008).

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging type of carbon nanomaterial of quantum size (Himaja et al., 2015). Since their serendipitous discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), CDs have attracted intense interest because of their fascinating properties such as green synthesis (Wang et al., 2010; Yu et al., 2015), stable photoluminescence (PL) (Hu et al., 2015), high electrochemical activity, excellent aqueous solubility (Mosconi et al., 2015), easy functionalization, low toxicity (Baker and Baker, 2010) and good biocompatibility (Weng et al., 2015). CDs are benign alternatives to conventional semiconducting quantum dots, so they have been widely applied in bioimaging (Li et al., 2012a, 2012b; Zhao et al., 2015), but have rarely been used in sensing (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011). Although some FL sensors based on CDs have been reported, these sensors are mainly for detection of metal ions and inorganic anions like Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cr6+, Ag+, CN−, F−, and
NO 2 -
(Dong et al., 2012; Gogoi et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Qu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2012).

The main obstacles in the development of CDs-based sensors are associated with immobilization of CDs in a suitable matrix that allows the CDs to retain their PL properties and analyte permeation, and prevents CDs leaching. The selectivity and sensitivity of CDs sensors also remain underdeveloped. Silica-based MIPs may be an ideal matrix to efficiently protect the fluorescent CDs and also selectively bind template molecules. However, to strengthen the interaction between CDs and a silica-based matrix, the CDs need to be modified with organosilane before polymerization, which means that polymer preparation requires at least two steps.

In this paper, we introduce a novel methodology based on the NHSG process and molecular imprinting technology to fabricate CDs directly encapsulated in a silica MIP in a one-pot reaction. The high selectivity of the MIP is combined with the stable FL of CDs to realize simple, sensitive and selective detection of ST. Considering the high price and toxicity of ST, we use 1,8-dihydroxyanthraquinone as an alternative template to ST in the imprinting process. The synthesis process of CDs@MIP, is outlined in Fig. 1. CDs in the center of the sensor can be treated as antennas for selective recognition, signal amplification and optical readout, while the MIP coated on the surface of the CDs provides specific binding sites for ST. The FL of CDs@MIP is quenched when ST is present, and the mechanism of FL quenching is discussed. The extent of FL quenching is proportional to the concentration of ST in the sample. The properties of CDs@MIP are evaluated by a series of experiments, and it is also used to analyze the content of ST in grain samples.

N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS, 97%), γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTMS, 99%), quercetin (QC, 98%) and 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 99%) were purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Methacrylic acid (MAA, 99%) was obtained from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. 1,8-Dihydroxyanthraquinone (DT, 96%), ST (98%), AFB1 (98%), ochratoxin A (OTA, 99%) and zearalenone (ZEN, 98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA. Anhydrous citric acid (99%) and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. All reagents were of at least analytical grade and used without further treatment. Samples of rice, corn and millet were purchased from a local supermarket.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic measurements were performed using KBr pellets on a Bruker Tensor 37 FT-IR spectrometer (Germany). FL measurements were carried out on a Thermo Scientific Lumina FL spectrometer (America) equipped with a 1×1cm2 quartz cell. UV–vis spectra were measured on a Thermo Scientific Evolution 300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (America). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing the surface morphologies of the MIP and NIP were obtained using a Hitachi SU1510 electron microscope (Japan).

CDs were synthesized via the one-step reaction reported by Wang and his co-workers (Wang et al., 2011). AEAPMS (10mL) was placed in a 100-mL three-necked flask, and degassed with nitrogen for about 5min. The flask was heated to 240°C, and then anhydrous citric acid (0.5g) was quickly added to the flask with vigorous stirring. After cooling to room temperature, the CDs were purified by precipitation from petroleum ether three times, and then dispersed in a certain amount of ethanol for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silica-based hybrid MIP- and NIP-coated CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were coated with polymer by combining an imprinting technique with an NHSG process. DT (0.5mmol), CDs (100μL) and MAA (2.0mmol) were dissolved in a mixture of chloroform (4.0mL) and acetonitrile (1.0mL) in the dark and stirred for 1h. Cross-linker MPTMS (3.0mmol) and initiator AIBN (20.0mg) were added. The mixture was degassed for 10min by ultrasonication, purged with nitrogen for 15min, sealed and then polymerization was thermally initiated in a water bath at 55°C for 18h. After polymerization, the rigid template was crushed, sieved through a 200-mesh steel sieve, and then flushed with acetone to remove any unreacted reagents and template molecules. The template molecules were then removed by Soxhlet extraction in acetone/acetic acid (300mL, 8:2, v/v) until no analyte was detected by UV–vis spectrophotometry. CDs@MIP was obtained after drying in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 10h. NIP-coated CDs (CDs@NIP) was prepared using the same procedure without addition of the template molecule DT.

FL measurements were carried out with an excitation wavelength of 360nm, excitation and emission slit widths of 5nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 400V. To evaluate the adsorption and competitive recognition capacity of the synthesized materials, CDs@MIP or CDs@NIP (1mg) was thoroughly dispersed in chloroform solutions with different concentration of ST (2mL) in a 4-mL colorimetric tube at room temperature by shaking. After 4h, ST had bound to the binding sites of the polymer, quenching the FL of CDs@MIP or CDs@NIP. The FL intensity of each solution before and after binding ST was measured by FL spectroscopy.

CDs@MIP was used to determine the content of ST in corn, rice, and millet samples purchased from a local supermarket. First, ST was extracted from each sample using the Associate of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC) official method with slight modification as follows. The grain samples were ground into powders and sieved through an 80-mesh sieve. Finally ground test sample (10g) was placed in a 100-mL glass-stoppered Erlenmeyer flask. Acetonitrile (36mL) and 4% KCl solution (4mL) were added, and then the stopper was secured with masking tape. The flask was agitated for 30min in an oscillating shaker. Sodium sulfate (5g) was added, the flask was agitated for another 30min, and then stood for 5min. The mixture was then filtered through a short-stem funnel.

For FL analysis, the collected filtrate was evaporated to incipient dryness. The residue was dissolved in chloroform to give a volume of 2mL, filtered through a 0.45-μm nylon microporous membrane, and then stored in a clean glass bottle until FL analysis.

Prior to HPLC analysis, the collected filtrate was purified by SPE using a silica-gel column to eliminate matrix interference. After conditioning with hexane, the extraction solution was loaded into the SPE cartridge at a flow rate of 1.0mLmin−1. Impurities were removed with ether/hexane (6mL, 2:8, v/v), and then the target was eluted with ether/hexane (12mL, 3:7, v/v). The recovered fraction was dried by rotary evaporation and dissolved methanol/water (7:3, v/v) to give a volume of 2mL. The mixture was filtered through a 0.45-μm nylon microporous membrane before injection into the HPLC system.

The HPLC system consisted of a Waters 600E pump, UV–vis detector set at 246nm, 20-μL injection loop, a LC workstation for data collection and C18 reversed-phase column (5μm, 250×4.6mm2, Thermo Scientific). The mobile phase was methanol/water (70:30, v/v), and the flow rate was set at 0.7mLmin−1.

As illustrated in Fig. 1A, highly luminescent CDs were synthesized by an efficient one-step reaction using multi-aminosilane AEAPMS as a coordinating solvent. The synthesized organosilane-functionalized CDs were characterized with FT-IR and FL spectroscopies, the results of which are presented in Fig. 2. The synthesis of organosilane-functionalized CDs involved the decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid, which was accompanied by the formation of gas and condensable vapors. Correspondingly, there was surface passivation of the amine groups in AEAPMS through reaction with the carboxyl groups derived from the pyrolysis species (Wang et al., 2011). The acylation reaction in the surface passivation process was confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2A). Unlike pure AEAPMS (a), the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs (b) exhibited two characteristic peaks at 1650cm−1 and 1565cm−1 consistent with amide I and II vibrations, respectively. These typical signals of a C=ONR vibration indicate the successful surface passivation reaction of CDs. In addition, some similar peaks in their spectrums were observed, including the stretching vibrations of C–OH at 3371cm−1 and C–H at 2930cm−1 and 2880cm−1, stretching vibrations of C–O–Si at 1080cm−1, and bending vibrations of C=O at 1480cm−1 (Yan et al., 2014). The CDs show an obvious absorption peak centered at 360nm in their UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2B). The CDs exhibit strong blue PL at 460nm when excited at 340–400nm. PL emission strongly depended on excitation wavelength; as the excitation wavelength was increased, the emission of the CDs shifted to longer wavelength together with a marked decrease in photoluminescence intensity and broadening of its full width at half-maximum. This excitation-dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity is an characteristic feature of the PL of CDs as previously reported by Sun et al. and others (Hu et al., 2009; Sun et al., 2006; Zhao et al., 2008), it arose not only from the optical selection of differently sized nanoparticles (quantum effect), but also primarily from different emissive traps present on their surface owing to the presence of functional groups (Li et al., 2012a, 2012b; Mitra et al., 2012). At certain excitation wavelengths, some emissive sites would be excited and fluoresce, resulting in the excitation-dependent behaviors of emission spectra (Bao et al., 2011). The Raman spectrum of the CDs contained only a weak G band at 1557cm−1 and no peak around 1360cm−1, revealing their amorphous nature (Fig. S2).

We proposed a one-pot synthetic strategy to prepare a silica-based hybrid MIP doped with CDs by the NHSG route, as illustrated in Fig. 1B. In this approach, MAA and MPTMS respectively acted as the functional monomer and cross-linker. MAA is a carboxylic acid that catalyzed the NHSG condensation of MPTMS and the organosilane-functionalized CDs to form a Si–O–Si framework containing CDs. At the same time, the polymerization of the carbon–carbon double bonds of MAA and MPTMS was initiated in the presence of radical initiator AIBN (Wang et al., 2006) As a result, the highly luminescent CDs were embedded in a silica-based network. Specific recognition sites for DT or ST were generated after removal of the template molecules.
Fig. 3(A and B) present SEM images showing the surface morphology of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP. Both samples had a rough surface and relatively narrow size distribution centered around 75μm.

FT-IR spectra of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP are illustrated in Fig. 3(C). The strong broad peaks observed at 1667cm−1 (C=O) and 3371cm−1 (O–H) originated from the stretching vibrations of DT (see spectrum (a)). These characteristic peaks were also observed for MIP before extraction (b), but then nearly disappeared after solvent elution (c). This indicates that DT molecules have been successfully encompassed in the silica matrix by the polymerization reaction and then effectively washed away by the extraction process. The peaks at 1658cm−1 and 1553cm−1 consistent with amide I and amide II vibrations, respectively, reveal that CDs have been successfully incorporated into the polymers. The peak around 1060cm−1 is assigned to Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching, and those at 779cm−1 and 453cm−1 are attributed to the Si–O vibrations, indicating the formation of a Si–O–Si framework. All of the above results further confirm that the imprinted polymer films have been successfully grafted onto the surface of the CDs. In addition, the FT-IR spectra of MIP and NIP showed similar characteristic peaks (c and d); this suggests that their compositions are similar, and that most template molecules were removed by extraction.

To study the effect of silica coating on the optical properties of CDs, the FL excitation and emission spectra of CDs@MIP were measured (Fig. 3(D)). CDs@MIP exhibited a FL emission peak at 460nm when excited at 360nm, which is in accordance with the emission of the bare CDs. This suggests that the PL properties of the CDs are not restricted by the silica-based MIP layer, which is logical because silica is optically transparent and chemically inert (Sun et al., 2007).

CDs@MIP was then used for detection of ST. As shown in Fig. 4(A), when DT is present in the CDs@MIP matrix (d), the FL of CDs@MIP is sharply quenched; after template extraction (b), the FL intensity of CDs@MIP is restored to 90% that of CDs@NIP (a). More importantly, ST also quenches the FL of CDs@MIP (c). Furthermore, the degree of quenching is affected by the concentration of ST ( Fig. 5C). According to these phenomena, a mechanism of fluorescence quenching is proposed. Because the absorption band of ST does not overlap with the emission spectrum of CDs@MIP, energy transfer can be excluded. The UV–vis adsorption band of ST is close to the band gap of CDs@MIP (Fig. S3), so the charges in the conduction band of CDs@MIP can be transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of ST. Therefore, electron transfer from CDs@MIP (donor) to ST (acceptor) is suggested as the possible mechanism of FL quenching. This indicates that CDs@MIP could bind ST through hydrogen bonding and the bound ST accepted electrons from CDs in the MIP matrix, quenching its FL.

The FL stability of CDs@MIP is an important evaluation indicator to measure if the material can be used in actual detection, so an experiment involving detection of the FL intensity of CDs@MIP (0.5mgmL−1) every hour for 13h was conducted (Fig. 4(B)). A relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.29% was obtained, which indicates that CDs@MIP exhibits excellent stability against photobleaching.

The time response of the FL quenching of CDs@MIP to ST (1.0mgL−1) was measured. The resulting adsorption kinetic plots (Fig. S4) reveal that after 2h, the FL intensity of CDs@MIP decreased sharply. Over time, this change became smaller, and then a constant value was reached after 4h. Compared with CDs@MIP, the changes of FL intensity of CDs@NIP were obviously weaker, which originates from the lack of specific binding between ST and CDs@NIP.

The selectivity and sensitivity of CDs@MIP towards the target ST was also investigated. We used DT, QC, OTA, AFB1 and ZEN with similar structures to that of ST to conduct selectivity and competitive blinding experiments; the results are presented in Fig. 5(A) and (B), respectively. Fig. 5(A) shows the FL responses (F 0/F) of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP to DT, QC, ST, OTA, ZEN and AFB1 (0.5mgL−1). DT, QC and ST obviously quenched the FL of CDs@MIP (F 0/F−1=0.306, 0.270, 0.230), which are respectively 2.83, 2.70 and 2.42 times the intensity quenching of CDs@NIP. Owing to using of DT as template in imprinting process, the selectivity of CDs@MIP for DT and QC is higher than ST. However, in most cases, DT and QC don't exist together with ST in samples; even coexistence, after the extraction process, the interference from them will be greatly reduced, or totally eliminated. Conversely, OTA, ZEN and AFB1, which usually exist with ST in contaminated grains, had weak effect on the FL of CDs@MIP. This is because the double screening function of MIP and CDs meant that CDs@MIP showed higher selectivity for ST than other interferent components (OTA, ZEN and AFB1). It was worth noting that ZEN and AFB1 caused a slight enhancement of FL intensity, possibly because of their autofluorescence.

To investigate the resistance of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP to interference from analogs, we carried out an experiment using ZEN as a competitor. Fig. 5(B) displays the binding capacity of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP toward ST in the presence of different concentrations of ZEN. As the concentration of ZEN increased from 0.5 to 2.0mgL−1, the degree of FL quenching (F 0/F−1) of CDs@MIP decreased slightly from 0.228 to 0.222. Meanwhile, F 0/F−1 of CDs@NIP was similar, changing from 0.093 to 0.085. These results indicate that the binding of ST by CDs@MIP was almost free from interference from its analog ZEN, even at high concentration. The high specificity of CDs@MIP for ST is attributed to the specific shape of the cavities produced in the imprinting process. The selectivity of MIPs often depends on the degree of similarity of molecule size, shape, and functionality between the imprinted molecule and analyte. Neglecting molecular size, we find that DT is far more similar to ST than to OTA, ZEN and AFB1 in terms of molecular shape and functional groups. This is therefore considered the main reason for the high selectivity and specificity of CDs@MIP for ST. As a result, CDs@MIP is an ideal candidate for use as a sensor for ST.

The effect of ST concentration on the FL intensity of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP is shown in Fig. 5(C and D). As the concentration of ST increases, the FL intensity decreases notably. The FL quenching of the systems followed the Stern–Volmer equation (Wang et al., 2009),
(1)
F
0
/ F = K
s v
C
ST
+ 1
where F 0 and F are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of ST, respectively, K sv is the quenching constant of the quencher, and C ST is the concentration of ST. Here, K sv is equal to the imprinting factor and is used to evaluate the selectivity of the materials.

From the calibration plots depicted in the insets of Fig. 5(C and D), the corresponding equations of F 0/F=0.387C ST+1.048 and F 0/F=0.053C ST+1.065 were obtained for CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP, respectively, over a concentration range of 0.05–2.0mgL−1 with correlation coefficients of 0.9966 and 0.9989, respectively. The detection limit of ST by CDs@MIP was calculated to be 19.0μgL−1 (S/N=3), and the precision (RSD) for five replicate detections of ST (0.10mgL−1) was 2.31%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ST in real samples using CDs@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy and practicality of the constructed CDs@MIP FL sensor, spiking and recovery experiments were conducted using millet, rice and maize samples. CDs@MIP was added to each sample extract, and then reacted for 4h at room temperature. For spiked levels of ST of 50, 100 and 200μgkg−1, recoveries ranging from 92.0% to 102.5% for three samples were obtained using CDs@MIP, as shown in Table 1. SPE–HPLC was used to detect ST in equivalent spiked samples, giving recoveries of 83.7–90.8% (Table S1). The higher recoveries obtained by the developed CDs@MIP FL sensor than SPE–HPLC could be attributed to its simple sample pre-treatment. These results show that CDs@MIP should be useful in actual testing.

We constructed a highly sensitive and selective FL sensor by an efficient one-pot reaction. The sensor integrated the advantages of both CDs and MIPs, so it had the functions of signal amplification and optical readout, as well as specific recognition. When the sensor was used to determine the content of ST in real samples, satisfactory results were obtained. This study provides a new possible modality for analysis of mycotoxins, and also furthers the application of CDs in optical and chemical sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of People's Republic of China, China (Project no. 2012AA101602), National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Project no. 31225021), Special Fund for Agro-scientific Research in the Public Interest, China (Project no. 201203069), and Innovation Talents of Science and Technology Plan of Yunnan Province (Project no. 2012HA009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:1016/j.bios.2015.10.072.
